translation.definition_en: Used to refer to a person, thing, or idea that is specific or already known to the listener.
translation.definition_ar: تُستخدم للإشارة إلى شخص أو شيء أو فكرة محددة أو معروفة مسبقًا للمستمع.
translation.usage_notes:
Arabic speakers often make two common mistakes with 'the': 
1. Adding it to general abstract nouns because Arabic uses 'ال' (e.g., saying 'The life is beautiful' instead of 'Life is beautiful'). 
2. Forgetting it with singular countable nouns when referring to a specific item (e.g., saying 'Open door' instead of 'Open the door').

examples.sentence_en: Please close {the} door when you leave.
I looked up at {the} moon.
He is {the} best student in our class.
I bought a book and a pen. {The} book is very interesting.
She plays {the} piano beautifully.

questions.prompt_text: the
questions.explanation: 'The' is the definite article in English, equivalent to 'الـ' (أل التعريف) in Arabic.

questions.prompt_text: الـ
questions.explanation: The Arabic definite article 'الـ' translates to 'the' in English.

questions.prompt_text: Used to refer to a person, thing, or idea that is specific or already known.
questions.explanation: This is the definition of the definite article 'the'.

questions.prompt_text: Look at ___ moon! It is so bright tonight.
questions.explanation: We use 'the' with unique objects in nature, like 'the sun', 'the moon', and 'the sky'.

questions.prompt_text: I saw a dog and a cat. ___ dog was very big.
questions.explanation: When we mention a noun for the second time, it becomes specific, so we use 'the'.

questions.prompt_text: Rabat is ___ capital of Morocco.
questions.explanation: There is only one capital of Morocco, so it is specific. We must use 'the'.

questions.prompt_text: She is ___ most intelligent student in her group.
questions.explanation: We always use 'the' before superlative adjectives (e.g., the best, the most).

questions.prompt_text: Can you pass me ___ salt, please?
questions.explanation: We use 'the' because both the speaker and the listener know exactly which salt is being referred to (the one on the table).

questions.prompt_text: I love playing ___ guitar in my free time.
questions.explanation: In English, we use 'the' before the names of musical instruments when talking about playing them generally.

questions.prompt_text: The sun is very hot today.
questions.explanation: Here, 'the' is used because there is only one sun in our solar system.

distractors.text: Referring to a previously mentioned object

questions.prompt_text: The man in the blue shirt is my professor.
questions.explanation: Here, 'the' is used to point out exactly which man the speaker is talking about.

distractors.text: Referring to a previously mentioned object

questions.prompt_text: The rich should help the poor.
questions.correct_answer: Referring to a whole group of people
questions.explanation: When 'the' is used with an adjective like 'rich' or 'poor', it refers to the entire group of people who share that quality.

distractors.text: Referring to a previously mentioned object

questions.prompt_text: I read the book you recommended.
questions.correct_answer: Referring to a specific, known item
questions.explanation: Here, 'the' is used because the listener already knows which book is being discussed (the one they recommended).

distractors.text: Referring to a whole group of people

questions.prompt_text: I need to buy ___ umbrella because it is raining.
questions.explanation: Since 'umbrella' starts with a vowel sound and is not specific yet, we use 'an', not 'the' or 'a'.

questions.prompt_text: ___ cars over there are very expensive.
questions.explanation: We use 'those' to point to plural specific things that are far away.

questions.prompt_text: I don't have ___ money left in my wallet.
questions.explanation: In negative sentences with uncountable nouns, we use 'any', not 'the' or 'some'.

questions.prompt_text: ___ apples on this table are for you.
questions.explanation: We use 'these' to point to plural specific things that are near us.

translation.definition_en: To exist, live, or have a certain state, quality, or identity.
translation.definition_ar: أن يوجد، يعيش، أو يكون لديه حالة أو صفة أو هوية معينة.
translation.usage_notes: A major challenge for Arabic speakers: Arabic nominal sentences (جملة اسمية) do not use a visible verb in the present tense (e.g., 'أنا طالب' literally translates to 'I student'). In English, you MUST use the verb 'to be' (I am a student). Do not drop 'am/is/are'!

examples.sentence_en: I {am} a university student in Casablanca.
examples.sentence_ar: أنا {أكون} طالبًا جامعيًا في الدار البيضاء.

examples.sentence_en: She {is} very happy today.
They {were} at the library yesterday afternoon.
examples.sentence_ar: هم {كانوا} في المكتبة بعد ظهر أمس.

examples.sentence_en: He has {been} working on this project for a month.
examples.sentence_ar: لقد {كان} يعمل على هذا المشروع منذ شهر.

examples.sentence_en: You need to {be} careful when crossing the street.
examples.sentence_ar: تحتاج أن {تكون} حذراً عند عبور الشارع.

questions.prompt_text: be
questions.explanation: The verb 'to be' translates to 'يكون' in Arabic, expressing existence or state.

questions.prompt_text: يكون
questions.explanation: The Arabic word 'يكون' translates to the English verb 'to be'.

questions.prompt_text: To exist, live, or have a certain state, quality, or identity.
questions.explanation: This is the definition of the verb 'to be'.

questions.prompt_text: My brother ___ a doctor at the local hospital.
questions.explanation: For singular third-person subjects (he/she/it) in the present tense, we use 'is'.

questions.prompt_text: We ___ very tired after the long trip yesterday.
questions.explanation: Because the sentence is in the past ('yesterday') and the subject is plural ('We'), we use 'were'.

questions.prompt_text: I ___ going to call you when you arrived.
questions.explanation: For the past continuous with the subject 'I', we use 'was'.

questions.prompt_text: They ___ studying for their exams right now.
questions.explanation: For the present continuous with the plural subject 'They', we use 'are'.

questions.prompt_text: She has ___ waiting for the bus for 30 minutes.
questions.explanation: In the present perfect continuous tense ('has been doing'), we use the past participle 'been'.

questions.prompt_text: You must ___ quiet in the library.
questions.explanation: After a modal verb like 'must', we always use the base form of the verb, which is 'be'.

questions.prompt_text: I am a student at Hassan II University.
questions.explanation: Here, 'am' connects the subject 'I' to the noun 'student' to state an identity.

distractors.text: Acting as an auxiliary for continuous tense
Acting as an auxiliary for passive voice

questions.prompt_text: The keys are on the kitchen table.
questions.explanation: In this sentence, 'are' is used to tell us where the keys exist physically.

distractors.text: Acting as an auxiliary for continuous tense
Acting as an auxiliary for passive voice

questions.prompt_text: She is reading a book right now.
questions.correct_answer: Acting as an auxiliary for continuous tense
questions.explanation: Here, 'is' has no meaning on its own. It is a helping verb used with 'reading' to form the present continuous tense.

distractors.text: Acting as an auxiliary for passive voice

questions.prompt_text: The window was broken by the wind.
questions.correct_answer: Acting as an auxiliary for passive voice
questions.explanation: In this sentence, 'was' is used with the past participle 'broken' to form a passive sentence (the window didn't break itself).

distractors.text: Acting as an auxiliary for continuous tense

questions.prompt_text: I ___ very happy to meet you today.
questions.explanation: The correct form of 'to be' for the first-person singular 'I' in the present tense is 'am'.

questions.prompt_text: The children ___ playing outside when it started raining.
questions.explanation: The subject 'children' is plural, and the action happened in the past ('started'), so we use 'were'.

questions.prompt_text: He is ___ very difficult today.
questions.explanation: We use 'is being' + adjective to describe someone's temporary behavior right now.

questions.prompt_text: Have you ever ___ to Paris?
questions.explanation: In the present perfect tense ('Have you ever...'), we use the past participle 'been'.

translation.definition_en: Used to join words, phrases, or parts of sentences together.
translation.definition_ar: تُستخدم لربط الكلمات أو العبارات أو أجزاء من الجمل معًا.
translation.usage_notes: A very common mistake for Arabic speakers is repeating 'and' in a list (e.g., 'I bought apples and bananas and oranges' - اشتريت تفاحاً وموزاً وبرتقالاً). In English, we use commas for lists and only put 'and' before the very last item: 'I bought apples, bananas, and oranges'.

examples.sentence_en: My brother {and} I are going to the university.
She speaks Arabic, French, {and} English.
He opened the door {and} walked inside.
The exam was long {and} difficult.
Work hard {and} you will pass the module.

questions.prompt_text: and
questions.explanation: The conjunction 'and' is used to connect things, which translates to 'و' in Arabic.

questions.prompt_text: و
questions.explanation: The Arabic conjunction 'و' translates to 'and' in English.

questions.prompt_text: Used to join words, phrases, or parts of sentences together.
questions.explanation: This is the definition of the conjunction 'and'.

questions.prompt_text: I like to drink tea ___ coffee in the morning.
questions.explanation: We use 'and' to join two similar nouns in an affirmative sentence.

questions.prompt_text: We bought tomatoes, onions, ___ potatoes from the souk.
questions.explanation: In a list of three or more items, 'and' is placed only before the final item.

questions.prompt_text: My friend ___ I are studying for the final exams.
questions.explanation: We use 'and' to connect two subjects doing the same action.

questions.prompt_text: The weather today is sunny ___ warm.
questions.explanation: We use 'and' to connect two positive adjectives describing the same thing.

questions.prompt_text: He woke up early, took a shower, ___ went to work.
questions.explanation: We use 'and' before the last action in a sequence of events.

questions.prompt_text: Study hard every day, ___ you will get good grades.
questions.explanation: Here 'and' is used after an imperative (command) to show the positive result that will follow.

questions.prompt_text: I bought a pen and a notebook.
questions.correct_answer: Adding an item to a group or list
questions.explanation: In this sentence, 'and' simply adds one noun to another.

questions.prompt_text: He fell down and broke his arm.
questions.explanation: Here, 'and' means 'and then'—one action happened, followed by another.

distractors.text: Adding an item to a list

questions.prompt_text: Eat your vegetables and you can have dessert.
questions.explanation: In this context, 'and' means 'if you do this, then that will happen'.

distractors.text: Adding an item to a group or list

questions.prompt_text: The new teacher is young and energetic.
questions.explanation: Here, 'and' connects two descriptions of the same person.

distractors.text: Adding an item to a list

questions.prompt_text: Can you please ___ my name to the list?
questions.explanation: We need the verb 'add' (to include), not the conjunction 'and'. They sound similar but have different functions.

questions.prompt_text: The ___ of a new library will help the students.
questions.explanation: We need the noun form 'addition' after 'The'.

questions.prompt_text: She is a great student. ___, she is an excellent athlete.
questions.explanation: At the beginning of a new sentence followed by a comma, we use the adverb 'Additionally' to add information.

questions.prompt_text: Two ___ two equals four.
questions.explanation: In simple math phrasing, 'and' acts as a conjunction connecting the two numbers (meaning plus).

forms.note: indefinite article: a (used before consonant sounds)

translation.definition_en: Used before a singular noun to refer to any one thing or person, not a specific one.
translation.definition_ar: تُستخدم قبل الاسم المفرد للإشارة إلى أي شيء أو شخص واحد، وليس شيئًا محددًا.
translation.usage_notes: Arabic does not have an indefinite article (we just say 'كتاب' for 'a book'). Because of this, Moroccan students often forget to use 'a' or 'an' before singular countable nouns in English. Remember: 'I am a student', not 'I am student'. Also, use 'a' before consonant SOUNDS, even if the word starts with a vowel letter (like 'a university').

examples.sentence_en: I need to buy {a} new phone.
My sister is {a} doctor.
We stayed in {a} hotel near the beach.
examples.sentence_ar: أقمنا في فندق بالقرب من الشاطئ.

examples.sentence_en: She studies at {a} university in Rabat.
examples.sentence_ar: هي تدرس في جامعة في الرباط.

examples.sentence_en: Take this medicine twice {a} day.
examples.sentence_ar: تناول هذا الدواء مرتين في اليوم.

questions.prompt_text: a
questions.explanation: In English, 'a' is an indefinite article. Arabic doesn't have an exact word for it, but it indicates a singular, non-specific noun.

questions.prompt_text: أداة تنكير (للمفرد)
questions.explanation: The English indefinite article used before consonant sounds is 'a'.

questions.prompt_text: Used before a singular noun to refer to any one thing or person, not a specific one.
questions.explanation: This is the definition of the indefinite article 'a'.

questions.prompt_text: My father works as ___ teacher at the high school.
questions.explanation: In English, we must use 'a' or 'an' before singular professions. Since 'teacher' starts with a consonant, we use 'a'.

questions.prompt_text: I would like to buy ___ car next year.
questions.explanation: We use 'a' to refer to one non-specific item (any car, not a specific one yet).

questions.prompt_text: He is driving ___ very fast car.
questions.explanation: Even though 'fast' is an adjective, it starts with a consonant, so we use 'a' before the adjective-noun combination.

questions.prompt_text: My brother studies at ___ university in Marrakesh.
questions.explanation: Although 'university' starts with the vowel letter 'u', it is pronounced with a 'y' sound (/juː/). Therefore, we use 'a', not 'an'.

questions.prompt_text: I go to the gym three times ___ week.
questions.explanation: In expressions of frequency, 'a' means 'per' or 'each' (e.g., once a day, twice a week).

questions.prompt_text: Can I have ___ cup of tea, please?
questions.explanation: We use 'a' before singular countable nouns, like 'cup'.

questions.prompt_text: I bought a car yesterday.
questions.explanation: The speaker is mentioning the car for the first time, so it is just 'one car' out of many in the world.

distractors.text: Referring to a specific, known item
Indicating a unique object in nature

questions.prompt_text: She is a nurse.
questions.explanation: In English, we must use 'a' or 'an' when stating someone's job or profession.

distractors.text: Referring to a specific, known item
Indicating a unique object in nature

questions.prompt_text: Take this medicine three times a day.
questions.explanation: In phrases of frequency, 'a' means 'per' (e.g., three times per day).

distractors.text: Referring to a specific, known item
Indicating a unique object in nature

questions.prompt_text: A cheetah can run very fast.
questions.correct_answer: Referring to any member of a species generally
questions.explanation: Here, 'a' means 'any cheetah in general', not one specific animal.

distractors.text: Referring to one specific, known item
Indicating a unique object in nature

questions.prompt_text: He is eating ___ apple for breakfast.
questions.explanation: Because 'apple' starts with a vowel sound, we must use 'an' instead of 'a'.

questions.prompt_text: She has ___ dog and two cats.
questions.explanation: Dog starts with a consonant sound, and it's singular, so we use 'a'.

questions.prompt_text: I waited at the station for half ___ hour.
questions.explanation: Although 'hour' starts with the consonant 'h', the 'h' is silent. It starts with a vowel sound, so we use 'an'.

questions.prompt_text: It is ___ honor to meet you, Professor.
questions.explanation: The word 'honor' has a silent 'h' and starts with a vowel sound, so we must use 'an'.

translation.definition_en: Expressing the relationship between a part and a whole, or showing possession, origin, or material.
translation.definition_ar: تُعبر عن العلاقة بين الجزء والكل، أو تدل على الملكية، أو الأصل، أو المادة المصنوع منها شيء ما.
translation.usage_notes: A major challenge for Arabic speakers: Arabic uses the 'Idafa' (إضافة) construct without a preposition (e.g., 'باب الغرفة' - door the room). In English, you must use 'of' to connect them: 'the door OF the room'. Also, do not confuse 'of' (one 'f', pronounced /v/) with 'off' (two 'f's, pronounced /f/).

examples.sentence_en: Rabat is the capital {of} Morocco.
Can I have a cup {of} tea, please?
examples.sentence_ar: هل يمكنني الحصول على كوب {من} الشاي، من فضلك؟

examples.sentence_en: This beautiful table is made {of} wood.
She is one {of} my best friends at the university.
examples.sentence_ar: إنها واحدة {من} أفضل أصدقائي في الجامعة.

examples.sentence_en: He took a picture {of} the Hassan II Mosque.

questions.prompt_text: of
questions.explanation: The preposition 'of' indicates relationship, possession, or material, often translating to 'مِن' or the Arabic Idafa construct.

questions.prompt_text: مِن (للجزء من الكل أو المادة)
questions.explanation: When expressing material ('made of') or part of a whole ('one of'), the translation is 'of'.

questions.prompt_text: Expressing the relationship between a part and a whole, or showing possession.
questions.explanation: This describes the primary function of the preposition 'of'.

questions.prompt_text: I drank a glass ___ water because I was thirsty.
questions.explanation: We use 'of' to connect a container or measurement (a glass) with its contents (water).

questions.prompt_text: The color ___ his new car is black.
questions.explanation: To show the relationship between 'color' and 'car' (the Arabic Idafa), we use 'of'.

questions.prompt_text: Marrakesh is a city in the south ___ Morocco.
questions.explanation: We use 'of' to show a geographic part-to-whole relationship.

questions.prompt_text: He is one ___ the smartest students in our class.
questions.explanation: The phrase 'one of' is used to identify a single person or thing out of a larger group.

questions.prompt_text: I bought a ring made ___ pure silver.
questions.explanation: When talking about the material something is constructed from, we use 'made of'.

questions.prompt_text: She showed me a picture ___ her family.
questions.explanation: To indicate the subject matter of a photo, painting, or story, we use 'of'.

questions.prompt_text: The door of the house is green.
questions.explanation: Here, 'of' links a part (the door) to the whole entity it belongs to (the house).

distractors.text: Indicating the material something is made from
Indicating the subject matter of an image
Indicating the agent of an action

questions.prompt_text: She drank two cups of coffee.
questions.explanation: In this sentence, 'of' connects the unit of measurement (cups) to the substance (coffee).

distractors.text: Indicating the material something is made from
Indicating the subject matter of an image
Indicating the agent of an action

questions.prompt_text: The traditional dress is made of silk.
questions.correct_answer: Indicating the material something is made from
questions.explanation: Here, 'of' is used after 'made' to tell us the physical material (silk) used to create the dress.

distractors.text: Indicating the subject matter of an image
Indicating the agent of an action

questions.prompt_text: This is a map of Africa.
questions.explanation: In this context, 'of' tells us what the map depicts or shows.

distractors.text: Indicating the material something is made from
Indicating the agent of an action

questions.prompt_text: Please turn ___ the television before you sleep.
questions.explanation: To stop an electronic device, we use the phrasal verb 'turn off' (with two Fs), not 'of'.

questions.prompt_text: My parents are very proud ___ me.
questions.explanation: The adjective 'proud' is always followed by the preposition 'of'.

questions.prompt_text: He took the book ___ the shelf.
questions.explanation: To indicate removing something from a surface, we use 'off' (two Fs), not 'of'.

questions.prompt_text: The students are afraid ___ failing the exam.
questions.explanation: The adjective 'afraid' is fixed with the preposition 'of'.

forms.note: pronoun: I (subject), me (object), my (possessive adj), mine (possessive pron)

translation.definition_en: Used by a speaker or writer to refer to himself or herself as the subject of a verb.
translation.definition_ar: يستخدمها المتحدث أو الكاتب للإشارة إلى نفسه كفاعل للفعل.
translation.usage_notes: Two major rules for Arabic speakers: 1. You cannot drop 'I' in English. In Arabic, you can say 'أدرس' (study) and the 'I' is understood. In English, you MUST say 'I study'. 2. The pronoun 'I' is ALWAYS capitalized in English, no matter where it is in the sentence.

examples.sentence_en: {I} am a student at the university.
{I} go to the gym every morning.
examples.sentence_ar: {أنا} أذهب إلى صالة الألعاب الرياضية كل صباح.

examples.sentence_en: My friend and {I} watched a movie yesterday.
{I} think we should study for the test.
examples.sentence_ar: أعتقد (أنـ{ـا}) أنه يجب علينا الدراسة للامتحان.

examples.sentence_en: When {I} was young, we lived in Tangier.
examples.sentence_ar: عندما كنت ({أنا}) صغيراً، عشنا في طنجة.

questions.prompt_text: I
questions.explanation: The subject pronoun 'I' translates to 'أنا' in Arabic.

questions.prompt_text: أنا
questions.explanation: The Arabic pronoun 'أنا' translates to the English subject pronoun 'I'.

questions.prompt_text: Used by a speaker to refer to himself or herself as the subject of a verb.
questions.explanation: This defines the first-person singular subject pronoun 'I'.

questions.prompt_text: ___ want to learn English to get a better job.
questions.explanation: We need a subject pronoun here. Since the verb is 'want' (not 'wants'), it must be 'I', 'you', 'we', or 'they'. Contextually, 'I' is the most natural for personal goals.

questions.prompt_text: Hello, ___ am very happy to meet you.
questions.explanation: The verb 'am' is only ever used with the subject pronoun 'I'.

questions.prompt_text: My brother and ___ went to the souk yesterday.
questions.explanation: When you are part of a compound subject (doing the action with someone else), you must use the subject pronoun 'I', not 'me'.

questions.prompt_text: ___ think the exam will be very difficult.
questions.explanation: We need the subject pronoun 'I' before the verb 'think' to express a personal opinion.

questions.prompt_text: When ___ was a child, we lived in a small village.
questions.explanation: The verb 'was' requires a singular subject. In the context of a personal memory, 'I' is the correct pronoun.

questions.prompt_text: Can ___ ask you a question about the homework?
questions.explanation: In a question form with a modal verb (Can), the subject pronoun 'I' comes after the modal.

questions.prompt_text: I am a medical student.
questions.explanation: Here, 'I' is used to talk about who the speaker is.

questions.prompt_text: I think this is the right answer.
questions.explanation: The speaker uses 'I' with 'think' to share their personal belief or opinion.

questions.prompt_text: I wake up at 7:00 AM every day.
questions.explanation: Here, 'I' is the subject of a routine or habit that happens regularly.

questions.prompt_text: Can I use your pen?
questions.explanation: In this question, 'I' is the subject of an action that the speaker wants permission to do.

questions.prompt_text: The teacher gave the book to ___.
questions.explanation: After a preposition like 'to', we must use the object pronoun 'me', not the subject pronoun 'I'.

questions.prompt_text: This is ___ new computer.
questions.explanation: Before a noun ('computer'), we use the possessive adjective 'my' to show ownership.

questions.prompt_text: That jacket is not yours, it is ___.
questions.explanation: At the end of a sentence without a noun following it, we use the possessive pronoun 'mine' instead of 'my'.

questions.prompt_text: I did all the homework by ___.
questions.explanation: To show that you did something alone without help, you use the reflexive pronoun 'myself' after 'by'.

forms.note: pronoun: you (subject/object), your (possessive adj), yours (possessive pron), yourself/yourselves (reflexive)

translation.definition_en: Used to refer to the person or people being spoken or written to.
translation.definition_ar: تُستخدم للإشارة إلى الشخص أو الأشخاص الذين يتم التحدث أو الكتابة إليهم.
translation.usage_notes: Arabic has different pronouns for speaking to a man (أنتَ), a woman (أنتِ), or a group (أنتم/أنتن). In English, 'you' is used for all of them! Also, 'you' can be the subject at the beginning of the sentence ('You are smart') AND the object at the end ('I saw you'), whereas Arabic uses attached suffixes for objects (رأيتـك).

examples.sentence_en: {You} are a very good student.
Are {you} all ready for the exam?
I will call {you} tomorrow morning.
This present is for {you}.
{You} should always wash your hands before eating.
examples.sentence_ar: يجب {عليك} دائماً غسل يديك قبل الأكل.

questions.prompt_text: you
questions.explanation: The pronoun 'you' is used for the second person in English, covering all singular and plural Arabic equivalents like أنت and أنتم.

questions.prompt_text: أنتَ / أنتم
questions.explanation: In English, 'you' translates to all forms of the second person (أنتَ, أنتِ, أنتم, أنتن).

questions.prompt_text: Used to refer to the person or people being spoken or written to.
questions.explanation: This describes the second-person pronoun, which is 'you'.

questions.prompt_text: ___ are my best friend.
questions.explanation: We need a subject pronoun that agrees with the verb 'are'. 'You' is the correct choice here.

questions.prompt_text: I want to ask ___ a question.
questions.explanation: Here we need an object pronoun after the verb 'ask'. 'You' works perfectly as an object.

questions.prompt_text: Did ___ finish the homework?
questions.explanation: In a question starting with 'Did', we need a subject pronoun. 'You' fits perfectly to ask the listener.

questions.prompt_text: This gift is for ___.
questions.explanation: After a preposition like 'for', we must use an object pronoun. 'You' functions as both subject and object.

questions.prompt_text: If ___ study hard, ___ will pass the exam.
questions.explanation: We need the subject pronoun 'you' in both clauses to make a direct conditional statement.

questions.prompt_text: Can ___ help me carry these heavy bags?
questions.explanation: In a request using 'Can', the subject pronoun 'you' is used to ask the listener to do something.

questions.prompt_text: You are a very kind person.
questions.correct_answer: Referring to a single listener as the subject
questions.explanation: Here, 'you' refers to one specific person being spoken to, acting as the subject of the sentence.

distractors.text: Referring to a group of listeners as the subject
Acting as the object of a verb
Acting as the object of a preposition

questions.prompt_text: Are you all coming to the party?
questions.correct_answer: Referring to a group of listeners as the subject
questions.explanation: The word 'all' shows that 'you' is being used to address multiple people.

distractors.text: Referring to a single listener as the subject
Acting as the object of a verb
Acting as the object of a preposition

questions.prompt_text: The teacher wants to see you after class.
questions.correct_answer: Acting as the object of a verb
questions.explanation: In this sentence, 'you' receives the action of the verb 'see'.

distractors.text: Referring to a single listener as the subject
Referring to a group of listeners as the subject
Acting as the object of a preposition

questions.prompt_text: You have to be careful when driving in the snow.
questions.explanation: Here, 'you' isn't just the listener; it means 'anyone' or 'people in general'.

distractors.text: Referring to a single listener as the subject
Referring to a group of listeners as the subject
Acting as the object of a verb
Acting as the object of a preposition

questions.prompt_text: Is this ___ book? I found it on the desk.
questions.explanation: Before a noun ('book'), we need the possessive adjective 'your' to show ownership.

questions.prompt_text: My car is blue, but ___ is red.
questions.explanation: When the noun is not mentioned after the pronoun, we use the possessive pronoun 'yours' (meaning 'your car').

questions.prompt_text: Did you make this cake all by ___?
questions.explanation: To ask if one person did something alone, we use the singular reflexive pronoun 'yourself'.

questions.prompt_text: Welcome to my home, guys! Please make ___ comfortable.
questions.explanation: Because the speaker is talking to a plural group ('guys'), the plural reflexive pronoun 'yourselves' is required.

translation.definition_en: To own, possess, or hold something; to eat or drink; or to be obliged to do something (have to).
translation.definition_ar: أن تملك أو تحوز شيئًا؛ أن تتناول طعامًا أو شرابًا؛ أو أن تكون ملزمًا بفعل شيء (have to).
translation.usage_notes: In Arabic, we often say 'عندي سيارة' (At me is a car) without a verb. In English, you MUST use the verb 'have' (I have a car). Also, 'have' is used for eating and drinking (e.g., 'have breakfast', 'have coffee'). Finally, 'have to' means 'must' (يجب أن).

examples.sentence_en: I {have} a new phone.
She {has} two brothers and one sister.
We usually {have} breakfast at 7:30 AM.
examples.sentence_ar: نحن عادة {نتناول} وجبة الإفطار في الساعة 7:30 صباحاً.

examples.sentence_en: You {have} to finish your homework before watching TV.
examples.sentence_ar: {يجب} عليك إنهاء واجبك المنزلي قبل مشاهدة التلفزيون.

examples.sentence_en: They {had} a great time at the festival yesterday.

questions.prompt_text: have
questions.explanation: The verb 'have' primarily means 'يملك' (to possess) or 'يتناول' (to eat/drink).

questions.prompt_text: يملك / لديه
questions.explanation: The Arabic concepts of possession (يملك, لديه, عنده) translate to the English verb 'have'.

questions.prompt_text: To own, possess, or hold something.
questions.explanation: This is the primary definition of the verb 'have'.

questions.prompt_text: I ___ a red car.
questions.explanation: For the subject 'I' in the present tense, we use 'have' to show possession.

questions.prompt_text: She ___ a big house in the city.
questions.explanation: For third-person singular subjects (he/she/it) in the present tense, 'have' changes to 'has'.

questions.prompt_text: We always ___ dinner at 8:00 PM.
questions.explanation: With meals (breakfast, lunch, dinner), we use 'have' to mean 'eat'. For the subject 'We', we use 'have'.

questions.prompt_text: You ___ to study hard if you want to pass the exam.
questions.explanation: The phrase 'have to' expresses obligation (meaning 'must').

questions.prompt_text: I ___ already finished my project.
questions.explanation: Here, 'have' is an auxiliary verb used with the past participle 'finished' to form the present perfect tense.

questions.prompt_text: They ___ a terrible headache yesterday.
questions.explanation: To talk about experiencing an illness or pain in the past ('yesterday'), we use the past tense 'had'.

questions.prompt_text: I have a new laptop.
questions.explanation: In this sentence, 'have' means that the laptop belongs to the speaker.

distractors.text: Acting as an auxiliary for a perfect tense

questions.prompt_text: Let's have coffee this afternoon.
questions.explanation: When 'have' is used with food or beverages, it means to consume them.

distractors.text: Acting as an auxiliary for a perfect tense

questions.prompt_text: You have to wear a seatbelt in the car.
questions.explanation: The phrase 'have to' means that something is required or mandatory.

distractors.text: Acting as an auxiliary for a perfect tense

questions.prompt_text: I have lived in Casablanca for five years.
questions.correct_answer: Acting as an auxiliary for a perfect tense
questions.explanation: Here, 'have' has no meaning on its own. It is a helping verb used with 'lived' to form the present perfect tense.

questions.prompt_text: My friend ___ a very nice apartment.
questions.explanation: Because 'My friend' is singular (he/she), we must use 'has' instead of 'have'.

questions.prompt_text: We ___ a great time at the beach last weekend.
questions.explanation: The sentence is in the past ('last weekend'), so the past tense form 'had' is required.

questions.prompt_text: I cannot answer the phone right now, I am ___ dinner.
questions.explanation: To form the present continuous tense ('am doing'), we use the -ing form 'having'.

questions.prompt_text: He doesn't ___ any brothers or sisters.
questions.explanation: After the auxiliary 'doesn't', we must use the base form of the verb, which is 'have' (not 'has').

translation.definition_en: Used to indicate direction, destination, recipient, or used before a base verb to show purpose or form the infinitive.
translation.definition_ar: تُستخدم للإشارة إلى الاتجاه، أو الوجهة، أو المتلقي، أو تُستخدم قبل الفعل الأساسي لإظهار الغرض أو لتكوين المصدر.
translation.usage_notes:
Two major rules for Moroccan students: 
1. Purpose: To say 'pour acheter' or 'باش نشري', use 'TO buy', NOT 'for buy'. 
2. Dependent prepositions: Always say 'listen TO me' (not 'listen me'). Also, 'to' is a preposition of direction ('go TO the university', not 'go IN the university').

examples.sentence_en: I go {to} the university every morning.
I want {to} learn English quickly.
Please give this book {to} your brother.
I went to the supermarket {to} buy some milk.
examples.sentence_ar: ذهبت إلى السوبر ماركت {لـ}شراء بعض الحليب.

examples.sentence_en: It is a quarter {to} five.

questions.prompt_text: to
questions.explanation: The word 'to' usually translates to 'إلى' for direction, 'لِـ' for a recipient, or 'أن' before a verb.

questions.prompt_text: إلى / أن (قبل الفعل)
questions.explanation: The Arabic words 'إلى' (direction) and 'أن' (before a verb) both translate to 'to' in English.

questions.prompt_text: Used to indicate direction, destination, or used before a base verb.
questions.explanation: This is the definition of the preposition and infinitive marker 'to'.

questions.prompt_text: I am traveling ___ Agadir tomorrow.
questions.explanation: With verbs of movement like 'travel' or 'go', we use 'to' to show the destination.

questions.prompt_text: I need ___ study for my math exam.
questions.explanation: After verbs like 'need' or 'want', we use 'to' + the base verb (the infinitive).

questions.prompt_text: Can you explain this lesson ___ me?
questions.explanation: When indicating the recipient of an explanation, we use 'to' (explain something TO someone).

questions.prompt_text: Please listen carefully ___ the instructions.
questions.explanation: The verb 'listen' must always be followed by 'to' when indicating what you are hearing.

questions.prompt_text: I went to the library ___ borrow a book.
questions.explanation: To express purpose (why you did something), use 'to' + base verb. Never use 'for' + base verb.

questions.prompt_text: The class is from 8:00 AM ___ 10:00 AM.
questions.explanation: When describing a range of time or distance, we use the structure 'from X to Y'.

questions.prompt_text: She walked to the train station.
questions.explanation: Here, 'to' shows the physical place where she is going.

distractors.text: Forming the infinitive of a verb
Indicating the recipient of an object
Showing the material of an object
Indicating the agent of an action

questions.prompt_text: I would love to travel the world.
questions.correct_answer: Forming the infinitive of a verb
questions.explanation: In this sentence, 'to' is used before the verb 'travel' to create the infinitive form.

distractors.text: Indicating the recipient of an object
Showing the material of an object
Indicating the agent of an action

questions.prompt_text: He sent a text message to his manager.
questions.correct_answer: Indicating the recipient of an object
questions.explanation: Here, 'to' shows who is receiving the text message.

distractors.text: Forming the infinitive of a verb
Showing the material of an object
Indicating the agent of an action

questions.prompt_text: Hurry up, it is ten to nine!
questions.explanation: When telling time, 'to' is used to mean the minutes remaining until the next hour (8:50).

distractors.text: Forming the infinitive of a verb
Indicating the recipient of an object
Showing the material of an object
Indicating the agent of an action

questions.prompt_text: I am going ___ the doctor tomorrow.
questions.explanation: We need the preposition 'to' for destination. Do not confuse it with 'too' (also/excessive) or 'two' (the number 2).

questions.prompt_text: They decided ___ buy a new house.
questions.explanation: The verb 'decide' is followed by the infinitive marker 'to'.

questions.prompt_text: She opened the window ___ let some fresh air in.
questions.explanation: To express the purpose of an action, we use 'to' before the base verb. 'For' is incorrect here.

questions.prompt_text: He apologized ___ the teacher for being late.
questions.explanation: The verb 'apologize' is followed by 'to' when indicating the person receiving the apology.

forms.note: pronoun: he (subject), him (object), his (possessive adj/pron), himself (reflexive)

translation.definition_en: Used as the subject of a verb to refer to a man, boy, or male animal that has already been mentioned or is easily identified.
translation.definition_ar: تُستخدم كفاعل للفعل للإشارة إلى رجل أو صبي أو حيوان ذكر تم ذكره بالفعل أو يسهل التعرف عليه.
translation.usage_notes: A very common mistake for Arabic speakers: Do NOT use 'he' for masculine objects. In Arabic, a book (كتاب) is masculine ('هو'), but in English, objects have no gender. Always use 'it' for objects. 'He' is ONLY for male people (and sometimes male pets).

examples.sentence_en: {He} is my older brother.
Where is Ahmed? {He} is at work.
{He} likes to play football on weekends.
examples.sentence_ar: {هو} يحب لعب كرة القدم في عطلات نهاية الأسبوع.

examples.sentence_en: If a student wants to succeed, {he} must study hard.
examples.sentence_ar: إذا أراد الطالب أن ينجح، يجب {عليه} (هو) أن يدرس بجد.

questions.prompt_text: he
questions.explanation: The subject pronoun 'he' translates to 'هو' in Arabic, referring to a male person.

questions.prompt_text: هو (للعاقل)
questions.explanation: The Arabic pronoun 'هو' for a male person translates to 'he'.

questions.prompt_text: Used as the subject of a verb to refer to a man or boy.
questions.explanation: This is the definition of the third-person singular masculine pronoun 'he'.

questions.prompt_text: My father is a teacher. ___ works at the high school.
questions.explanation: We use the subject pronoun 'He' to replace 'My father' in the second sentence.

questions.prompt_text: Where is Youssef? ___ is waiting outside.
questions.explanation: 'Youssef' is a male name, so we use 'He' as the subject pronoun.

questions.prompt_text: ___ doesn't like drinking coffee.
questions.explanation: The auxiliary 'doesn't' requires a third-person singular subject. 'He' fits perfectly.

questions.prompt_text: Is ___ your new manager?
questions.explanation: In a question with 'Is', we need a singular third-person subject pronoun like 'he'.

questions.prompt_text: Although ___ was tired, the boy finished his homework.
questions.explanation: The pronoun 'he' is used to refer forward to 'the boy' in the main clause.

questions.prompt_text: Everyone should bring ___ own laptop to the meeting.
questions.explanation: Wait, the target word is 'he' or its forms. 'His' is the possessive adjective form of 'he', often used generically after 'everyone' (though 'their' is also common today).

questions.prompt_text: He is my uncle.
questions.correct_answer: Referring to a specific male person
questions.explanation: In this sentence, 'he' clearly identifies a male family member.

distractors.text: Referring to a specific female person
Referring to a group of people
Acting as the object of the sentence

questions.prompt_text: A good student makes sure he studies every day.
questions.correct_answer: Referring to a generic person of unknown gender
questions.explanation: Historically and formally, 'he' is sometimes used to refer to any person (like 'a student') regardless of actual gender.

distractors.text: Referring to a specific male person
Referring to a specific female person
Referring to a group of people
Acting as the object of the sentence

questions.prompt_text: Is he coming to the match tonight?
questions.correct_answer: Asking about a male person's actions
questions.explanation: Here, 'he' is the subject of a question inquiring about a man's future plans.

distractors.text: Asking about a female person's actions
Referring to a group of people
Acting as the object of the sentence

questions.prompt_text: My dog is very smart; he knows many tricks.
questions.explanation: While animals are usually 'it', people often use 'he' or 'she' for pets when they know the animal's gender.

distractors.text: Referring to a specific male person
Referring to a group of people
Acting as the object of the sentence

questions.prompt_text: I saw ___ at the library yesterday.
questions.explanation: After the verb 'saw', we need the object pronoun 'him', not the subject 'he'.

questions.prompt_text: Omar forgot ___ keys on the table.
questions.explanation: Before a noun ('keys'), we use the possessive adjective 'his' to show ownership.

questions.prompt_text: That jacket is not mine, it is ___.
questions.explanation: At the end of a sentence without a noun, 'his' acts as a possessive pronoun (meaning 'his jacket').

questions.prompt_text: He repaired the broken computer all by ___.
questions.explanation: To show that a male subject did something alone without help, use the reflexive pronoun 'himself'.
